metadata.title: A quantum matter wave has two speeds — and the one you can see is the wrong one
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL=envelope/group velocity/particle; CRIMSON=phase crests/wrong speed; GOLD=reference pin/marker
metadata.note: Phase velocity v/2 vs group velocity v for a de Broglie matter wave. Beats derivation.

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Track the crests of a quantum matter wave. They move at half the electron's speed. Track the wave packet's envelope. It moves at the full speed. Same wave, same moment, two different speeds.

card.headline:
A quantum matter wave
has two speeds — and the
one you can see is wrong

beats.narration_text: A de Broglie wave is supposed to represent a moving electron. Track its wave crests: they advance at half the electron's speed. If the wave represents the particle, why is it moving at the wrong speed?

card.headline:
De Broglie wave crests
move at half the electron's
speed. Why?

beats.narration_text: For a non-relativistic free electron, kinetic energy equals p squared over 2m. Planck and de Broglie give omega equals hbar k squared over 2m. The phase velocity is omega over k — equal to hbar k over 2m, which is p over 2m, which is v over 2. The crests really do move at half speed.

A single infinite plane wave carries no position information. The electron is not in a plane wave — it is in a packet, a superposition of many k-values clustered around a central momentum. Watch what two nearby frequencies produce when added.

Add two waves with slightly different k and omega. The result: a fast carrier wave times a slow envelope. The carrier advances at the average phase velocity — still half the particle speed. But the envelope, the part you can localize, advances at d-omega over d-k — the group velocity.

For a de Broglie wave, d-omega over d-k equals hbar k over m, which is p over m, which is v — the full classical speed. The envelope moves at v. The crests move at v over 2. The particle is carried by the envelope, not by the crests.

Pin a marker to the envelope peak. Watch: the crests slide backward through the marker. They are overtaken. A detector that tracks crests would predict the electron arrives early — and at the wrong position. The group velocity is what experiments measure.

The phase velocity is real — it is the speed at which the pattern of constructive and destructive interference advances. But it carries no signal, no energy, no particle. The group velocity is the physical speed. The two are equal only in a non-dispersive medium.

An electron accelerated through 100 volts reaches roughly 6 million meters per second. Its de Broglie wave crests advance at 3 million meters per second. A detector 1 centimeter away clicks at a time set by the envelope, not the crests. The 2-to-1 speed ratio is fixed by the quadratic dispersion relation omega proportional to k squared.
Phase velocity omega over k equals v over 2. Group velocity d-omega over d-k equals v. The particle rides the envelope. The crests are not the particle.

card.headline:
Phase: ω/k = v/2.
Group: dω/dk = v.
Particle rides the envelope.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Quantum Mechanics: Math for Quantum Physics series.
